title: Collection generate: implementation
analysis: The evaluator already handles `generate` as a MethodCall on List values (crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs lines 562-580), invoked as `[].generate(n, |i| expr)`. There is one existing test (eval_method_generate_list) covering the basic happy path `[].generate(3, |i| i*2) -> [0, 2, 4]`. However, the task requires `List.generate(n, |i| expr)` as a 'static method / stdlib function', meaning it should be callable WITHOUT a List receiver. The approach: add `generate` as a FunctionCall handler in eval_expr (alongside `sample`, which similarly needs EvalContext for lambda application), and add comprehensive edge-case tests for both the new FunctionCall path and the existing MethodCall path. Note: `generate` CANNOT live in reify-stdlib/eval_builtin because it requires EvalContext for apply_lambda (same reason `sample` is in reify-expr). The compiler (not in scope) will fall through to FunctionCall for unknown function names, so `generate(n, fn)` will compile correctly. The result_type metadata may be wrong (defaults to first arg's type), but evaluation is correct regardless.

steps.description: Write failing test: `generate` as FunctionCall basic case. Construct CompiledExprKind::FunctionCall with ResolvedFunction{name:'generate'}, two args: Int(3) literal and lambda |i| i*2. Verify result is Value::List([Int(0), Int(2), Int(4)]). Test name: eval_fn_generate_basic.

Add 'generate' match arm to the FunctionCall dispatch in eval_expr (crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs ~line 86, after the gradient/divergence/curl arm). Pattern: `"generate" if evaluated_args.len() == 2 => { match &evaluated_args[0] { Value::Int(n) => { let results: Vec<Value> = (0..*n).map(|i| apply_lambda(&evaluated_args[1], &[Value::Int(i)], ctx)).collect(); Value::List(results) }, _ => Value::Undef } }`.

Write test: FunctionCall generate(0, |i| i) -> empty list []. Construct FunctionCall with Int(0) count and identity lambda. Verify result is Value::List(vec![]). Test name: eval_fn_generate_zero_count.
Verify zero-count works (Rust 0..0 range is empty, produces empty Vec). Should pass without changes.

Write test: MethodCall [].generate(Undef, |i| i) -> Undef. Use CompiledExpr::method_call with first arg being a ValueRef to an unresolved cell (producing Undef at eval time). Verify result is Value::Undef. Test name: eval_method_generate_undef_count.
Verify Undef count handling in MethodCall path. The match &args[0] { Value::Int(n) => ..., _ => return Value::Undef } already covers this. Should pass without changes.

Write test: MethodCall [].generate(-3, |i| i) -> empty list []. Negative count should produce an empty list via Rust range semantics (0i64..-3i64 is empty). Test name: eval_method_generate_negative_count.
Verify negative count produces empty list. Rust Range 0..-3 iterates zero times. Should pass without changes.
Write test: MethodCall [].generate(3.0, |i| i) -> Undef. Real (non-Int) count should return Undef since match on Value::Int excludes Value::Real. Test name: eval_method_generate_real_count.
Verify Real count returns Undef. The match on Value::Int in the generate handler falls through to _ => Value::Undef for Real values. Should pass without changes.
Write test: MethodCall [].generate(4, |i| i) identity lambda -> [0, 1, 2, 3]. Tests that indices 0..n-1 are correctly passed to the lambda. Test name: eval_method_generate_identity.
Verify identity lambda works. apply_lambda with identity body (just ValueRef to param) returns the Int index directly. Should pass without changes.
Write test: MethodCall generate with wrong arity (1 arg only - count but no lambda) -> Undef. Tests the args.len() != 2 guard. Test name: eval_method_generate_wrong_arity.
Verify wrong-arity guard works. The if args.len() != 2 { return Value::Undef } check at line 563 already handles this. Should pass without changes.

Write test: FunctionCall generate with Undef n -> Undef. The FunctionCall dispatch has automatic Undef short-circuit (lines 72-73), so Undef count never reaches the handler. Construct FunctionCall where first arg evaluates to Undef (ValueRef to missing cell). Test name: eval_fn_generate_undef_count.

Verify FunctionCall Undef propagation works. The automatic short-circuit `if evaluated_args.iter().any(|v| v.is_undef()) { return Value::Undef; }` fires before reaching the generate handler. Should pass without changes.

design_decisions.decision: Add generate as a FunctionCall handler in eval_expr rather than in reify-stdlib/eval_builtin
design_decisions.rationale: generate requires EvalContext for apply_lambda, which eval_builtin does not receive. The precedent is 'sample', which is similarly handled in eval_expr's FunctionCall match (lines 78-92) because it needs EvalContext for field lambda application.

design_decisions.decision: Keep existing MethodCall handler unchanged; add FunctionCall path alongside it
design_decisions.rationale: The MethodCall path ([].generate(n, fn)) already works and has a test. The FunctionCall path (generate(n, fn)) provides the 'static method / stdlib function' semantics the task requires, without needing a List receiver. Both paths coexist for maximum flexibility.

design_decisions.decision: Negative count produces empty list rather than Undef
design_decisions.rationale: Rust range 0..n where n<0 produces an empty iterator, giving Value::List(vec![]). This is consistent with generate(0, fn) -> [] and is the least-surprise behavior. The task does not specify negative-count semantics, and empty-list is a safe, non-error default.

design_decisions.decision: No changes to reify-stdlib despite it being listed in task modules
design_decisions.rationale: The stdlib crate's eval_builtin function does not receive EvalContext, which is required for lambda application via apply_lambda. All lambda-aware functions (map, filter, generate, fold, sample) are handled in reify-expr's eval_expr dispatch. Adding generate to stdlib would require an architectural change (passing EvalContext to eval_builtin) which is out of scope.

reuse.how: Copy the same pattern: add a match arm in the FunctionCall dispatch that checks function name and arity, then uses apply_lambda with EvalContext. The generate handler mirrors the sample handler structure.

The FunctionCall handler replicates the same core logic but without the obj/receiver check. Core logic: match Int count from first arg, iterate 0..count, apply_lambda for each index, collect into List.
Reuse these existing helpers to construct lambda CompiledExpr values for all generate tests. lambda_literal wraps make_value_lambda into a CompiledExpr::Literal.
Reuse for constructing MethodCall test expressions, following the pattern established in eval_method_generate_list and other collection tests.
Construct ResolvedFunction { name: 'generate', qualified_name: 'std::generate' } for FunctionCall test expressions, following the pattern from make_function_call (line 691).
